question_text: A Woodruff key is:
explanation: Answer: C | A Woodruff key has a semicircular (half-moon) shape. It sits in a circular keyway milled in the shaft and fits into a rectangular keyway in the hub. Woodruff keys are self-aligning and easy to install, making them common in automotive and light machinery applications. They are sized by a number system (e.g., #808 = 1/4 wide x 1 diameter).

question_text: A rigid coupling requires:
explanation: Answer: B | Rigid couplings (sleeve, clamp, or flanged types) transmit torque without any flexibility and require near-perfect shaft alignment  typically within 0.001 to 0.002 TIR. Any misalignment is transmitted directly as bending loads to the shafts and bearings. Rigid couplings are used where precise shaft positioning is required, such as multi-bearing shaft systems.

question_text: The tolerance for alignment depends on:
option_a: Speed, coupling type, and manufacturer specs
explanation: Answer: A | Shaft alignment tolerances depend primarily on operating speed (RPM), coupling type, and manufacturer specifications. Higher RPM requires tighter tolerances. For example, at 1800 RPM: typical acceptable angular misalignment is 0.002 per inch of coupling diameter; parallel offset < 0.003. At 3600 RPM, tolerances are roughly half these values. Always follow specific coupling manufacturer limits.

question_text: A spline connection provides:
option_b: High torque capacity and axial movement capability
explanation: Answer: B | Spline connections use multiple parallel keys (teeth) machined around the shaft circumference and a matching internal spline in the hub. They provide high torque capacity distributed across multiple teeth, and depending on the fit, allow controlled axial sliding movement. Common in automotive driveshafts, gearboxes, and tool holders.

question_text: When installing a horizontal roller chain drive, the tight (driving) side should be:
option_a: Either side depending on drive direction
explanation: Answer: C | For horizontal chain drives, the tight (driving) side should be on top. This allows the slack (loose) side on the bottom to sag naturally without creating excessive chain whip. The tensioner or idler, if used, is applied to the slack (bottom) side. This is standard per ANSI/ASME B29.1 chain drive installation guidelines.

question_text: V-belts are identified by their:
explanation: Answer: B | V-belts are identified by their cross-sectional profile (A, B, C, D, E for classical; 3V, 5V, 8V for wedge belts) which determines the top width and depth. The belt number also includes the length (e.g., B75 = B-section, 75 inches outside circumference). The cross-section must match the sheave groove profile for proper wedging action and power transmission.

question_text: The purpose of a tensioner in a chain drive is to:
explanation: Answer: C | A tensioner in a chain drive maintains proper chain tension as the chain wears and elongates over time. Proper tension prevents excessive sag on the slack side (which causes chain whipping and jump-off) while avoiding over-tensioning (which accelerates wear on pins, bushings, and sprocket teeth). Spring-loaded or adjustable idler sprockets are commonly used.

question_text: The cross dial (reverse dial indicator) alignment method uses:
option_b: One dial indicator spanning the coupling gap
option_d: Two dial indicators, one mounted on each shaft or coupling half, facing each other
explanation: Answer: D | The cross dial (reverse dial indicator) method uses two dial indicators, each mounted on one shaft or coupling half and reading on the other shaft. Both shafts are rotated together 360 degrees. This method measures both angular and parallel misalignment simultaneously and eliminates bracket sag errors more easily than rim-and-face methods.

question_text: A gear coupling:
explanation: Answer: D | A gear coupling uses internal and external gear teeth to transmit torque and accommodate angular misalignment (typically up to 1.5 degrees). The tooth profile allows slight angular offset between hubs. Gear couplings require periodic lubrication (grease or oil) and are used in heavy-duty, high-torque applications.

question_text: Torque is the product of:
explanation: Answer: C | Torque = Force x radius (moment arm). It is a rotational force measured in ft-lb or N-m. Example: a 50 lb force applied at a 2-foot radius produces 100 ft-lb of torque. In machinery, torque is what does the work of driving loads, while power (HP) describes how quickly that torque is delivered.

question_text: Chain pitch is measured:
option_a: From outside edge to outside edge
option_c: Along the outside of the chain
option_d: From center to center of adjacent pins
explanation: Answer: D | Chain pitch is the distance from the center of one pin to the center of the adjacent pin. It is the fundamental measurement for chain classification per ANSI/ASME B29.1 standards. Common pitches are 3/8, 1/2, 5/8, 3/4, and 1. Sprockets must match the chain pitch exactly.

question_text: Interference fit means:
explanation: Answer: D | An interference fit (also called a press fit or force fit) means the shaft diameter is larger than the hole diameter. Assembly requires pressing or heating and creates a compressive stress that locks the parts together without additional fasteners. The amount of interference (0.001 to 0.003 per inch of diameter is typical) determines the holding force.

question_text: The main advantage of the cross dial (reverse dial) alignment method over the rim-and-face method is:
option_c: It is faster to set up
option_d: It is less affected by axial shaft movement and provides more accurate angular measurement
explanation: Answer: D | The cross dial (reverse dial) method is less sensitive to axial shaft float than rim-and-face because both indicators measure radially. Axial movement affects both indicators equally and cancels out in calculations. It also gives a longer effective measurement span for angular misalignment, improving accuracy. Both methods require rotating shafts together.

question_text: Mechanical advantage in gearing:
explanation: Answer: B | Gearing trades speed for torque (or vice versa)  this is mechanical advantage. In a speed reduction (large driven gear), output speed decreases but output torque increases proportionally. In a speed increase (small driven gear), output speed increases but output torque decreases. Power remains constant (minus losses).

question_text: Gear tooth nomenclature includes:
explanation: Answer: C | Standard gear tooth nomenclature includes: pitch circle (theoretical circle where gears mesh), addendum (tooth height above pitch circle), dedendum (tooth depth below pitch circle), whole depth, face width, diametral pitch, and pressure angle. These terms are defined in AGMA and ISO gear standards.

question_text: When using cross dial method, sag must be:
explanation: Answer: D | Indicator bracket sag must be eliminated or compensated for in all dial indicator alignment methods. Gravity causes the bracket and indicator to deflect downward at the bottom position, giving a false reading. Sag is measured by supporting both ends of the bracket and noting the indicator change between top and bottom positions. This sag value is added to bottom readings in calculations.

question_text: The taper on a tapered key is typically:
explanation: Answer: B | Standard tapered keys have a taper of 1/8 inch per foot (1:96 taper) per ASME B17.1 standards. This taper allows the key to wedge tightly in the keyway when driven in, providing both torque transmission and axial retention. Gib head keys use the same taper but have a head for easier removal.

question_text: Horsepower (HP) is calculated using torque and speed as:
explanation: Answer: C | The formula HP = (Torque in ft-lb x RPM) / 5252 is derived from the definition of horsepower (33,000 ft-lb/min) and the conversion factor for rotational work. The constant 5252 = 33,000 / (2 x pi). Example: 100 ft-lb of torque at 1000 RPM = (100 x 1000) / 5252 = 19.04 HP.

question_text: If a driver gear has 20 teeth and driven gear has 40 teeth, the speed ratio is:
explanation: Answer: C | Speed ratio = driver teeth / driven teeth. With 20 driver teeth and 40 driven teeth, speed ratio = 20/40 = 1:2, meaning the driven gear runs at half the speed (speed reduction). Torque is inversely proportional: driven torque = driver torque x 2. This is a 2:1 gear reduction in torque terms.

question_text: Graphical alignment methods:
explanation: Answer: B | Graphical alignment methods (plotting dial indicator readings on graph paper scaled to represent the machinery dimensions) help the technician visualize where the machine feet need to be shimmed and by how much to correct both angular and parallel misalignment in both vertical and horizontal planes simultaneously. This reduces the number of alignment iterations needed.

question_text: Sprockets are classified by:
explanation: Answer: A | Sprockets are classified by pitch (distance between chain pin centers, e.g., 1/2 = #40 chain) and number of teeth, which together determine the sprocket's pitch diameter and speed ratio. Sprocket pitch must match chain pitch exactly. The number of teeth affects smoothness  more teeth on the driven sprocket gives smoother, quieter operation.

question_text: A flexible coupling:
explanation: Answer: A | A flexible coupling transmits torque while accommodating minor shaft misalignment (angular, parallel offset, and axial) through a flexible element such as rubber, elastomer, grid, or disc. It does not substitute for proper alignment but absorbs small residual misalignment and dampens vibration and shock loads.

question_text: Matched V-belts are required when:
option_c: Multiple belts run on same sheaves
explanation: Answer: C | Matched V-belts are required when multiple belts run in parallel on the same sheaves. Unmatched belts of the same nominal size can have small length differences, causing uneven load sharing where shorter belts carry most of the load and wear out prematurely. Matched sets are measured and coded to within tight length tolerances.

question_text: The purpose of a coupling is to:
explanation: Answer: B | A coupling connects two shafts end-to-end to transmit rotational power (torque) from a driver (motor, engine) to a driven machine (pump, gearbox, compressor). Couplings also isolate vibration and shock, accommodate thermal expansion, and allow disconnection for maintenance. The coupling type is selected based on misalignment tolerance, torque capacity, and operating speed.

question_text: In cross dial alignment, readings are taken at:
explanation: Answer: B | Cross dial alignment readings are taken at four clock positions: 12 o'clock (top), 3 o'clock, 6 o'clock (bottom), and 9 o'clock  representing 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270° of shaft rotation. The top and bottom readings determine vertical misalignment; the side readings determine horizontal misalignment. Both angular and offset components are calculated from these four readings.

question_text: In cross dial alignment, if both dial readings increase in the same direction:
explanation: Answer: B | In cross dial alignment, when both indicators show readings that change in the same direction (both increase or both decrease) as the shafts rotate, parallel (offset) misalignment is present. With angular misalignment only, readings would change in opposite directions. Analyzing the top/bottom and left/right readings separately identifies the type and magnitude of misalignment.

question_text: An Oldham coupling:
explanation: Answer: D | An Oldham coupling compensates for parallel (lateral) offset misalignment using a three-piece design: two flanged hubs with tongue projections at 90° to each other and a central disk with mating slots. The central disk slides laterally in both directions to accommodate offset while transmitting torque. It cannot compensate for angular misalignment.

question_text: Proper belt tension can be checked by:
explanation: Answer: A | Proper belt tension is verified by measuring deflection under a specified force at the midpoint of the longest span. The manufacturer specifies the acceptable deflection range (typically 1/64 per inch of span). Too much deflection indicates under-tension (slippage); too little indicates over-tension (premature bearing failure).

question_text: Sheave groove wear is indicated by:
option_a: Belt riding lower in the groove
explanation: Answer: A | Worn sheave grooves allow the belt to ride lower in the groove, reducing the contact arc and effective grip. A new belt in a worn sheave will bottom out in the groove, losing the wedging action that provides traction. Check with a groove gauge; replace sheave if worn beyond specification.

question_text: Shaft runout is measured with a:
explanation: Answer: B | Shaft runout (eccentricity or wobble) is measured with a dial indicator. The indicator tip contacts the shaft surface while the shaft is rotated; the total indicator reading (TIR) is the runout value. Acceptable runout varies by application: precision shafts typically < 0.001 TIR; general industrial < 0.002 TIR.

question_text: The main advantage of a synchronous belt drive over V-belts is:
explanation: Answer: B | Synchronous (timing) belts use teeth that mesh with grooved pulleys, providing a positive drive with zero slip. This makes them ideal for applications requiring precise speed ratios and timing, such as camshafts and CNC machines. V-belts rely on friction and can slip under overload or improper tension.

question_text: Cross dial alignment can detect:
explanation: Answer: B | The cross dial (reverse dial) method detects both angular misalignment and parallel offset misalignment simultaneously. Angular misalignment is identified by readings that change in opposite directions (one indicator increases while the other decreases). Parallel offset is identified by readings that change in the same direction. The graphical or mathematical method separates the two components.

question_text: The main cause of premature V-belt failure is:
explanation: Answer: D | Misalignment and improper tension are the leading causes of premature V-belt failure. Misalignment causes uneven wear on belt sidewalls and generates heat. Over-tensioning overloads bearings and stretches belts; under-tensioning causes slippage, glazing, and heat buildup. Both conditions dramatically reduce belt life.

question_text: A gear ratio of 2:1 means:
option_a: Both rotate at the same speed
option_b: The driver is twice as large
option_c: The driver rotates twice for each rotation of the driven
option_d: The driven rotates twice for each rotation of the driver
explanation: Answer: C | A gear ratio of 2:1 (driver:driven) means the driver gear rotates twice for every one rotation of the driven gear. The driven gear turns at half the speed but produces twice the torque. Speed ratio = driver teeth / driven teeth = driven RPM / driver RPM.

question_text: A key prevents:
option_c: Relative rotation between shaft and hub
explanation: Answer: C | A key is installed in matching keyways cut in both the shaft and hub to prevent relative rotation between them while transmitting torque. Keys are designed as shear elements and are sized to handle the torque load. They do not prevent axial (end-to-end) movement unless a setscrew or retaining ring is also used.

question_text: Set screw holding power depends on:
option_a: Thread size and cup point contact
explanation: Answer: A | Set screw holding power depends primarily on thread size (which determines the tightening torque and clamping force) and the point type. A cup point digs into the shaft surface to provide mechanical interlocking in addition to friction. Flat and oval points rely on friction only. Cone points are used in drilled indentations for the highest holding power.
